name: Yampolski
description: This specimen we imported from a Russian asylum, and are now showing him for the first time in this country.

name: Horn
description: Captured in the swamps of lower Georgia, and not yet civilized. Yes, he is the very image of 'Dan,' who escaped from the Museum the early part of the year, and we are inclined to believe they spring from the same stock.

name: Riley
description: These are positively the smallest men of the 20th Century. For five cents extra they will give you a complete history of their lives.

name: Blitch
description: These are positively the smallest men of the 20th Century. For five cents extra they will give you a complete history of their lives.

name: Herman R.
description: Not to see 'Herman R.' the Dog-faced boy, who occupies the small cage in front, —would be to miss the greatest curiosity on this side of the Atlantic.

name: Weems
description: Weems, the 'Musical Freak,' will now entertain you for a moment. We found both of these freaks in the mountains of North Georgia.

name: Jones, Col.
description: Col. Jones will give you one of his favorite speeches. We found both of these freaks in the mountains of North Georgia.

name: Spindle
description: In the next cage you will see 'Spindle,' the Fat Boy.

name: Small-o
description: On his right is 'Small-o,' the Living Skeleton.

name: Lowry, Jacob
description: Jacob Lowry sat in his boudoir at dusk. The last faint beams of the setting sun stole through the red curtains and enveloped the rouge pot and embroidery needle with a ruddy hue. His elbow was resting on the table and his eyes were riveted on the last 'Smart Set.' Slowly he raised his face from the printed page, and looked steadily out on the glowing West. Then with words of mingled pathos and resignation he muttered, 'She don't like me any more.'

name: Rewis, Millard
description: Millard Rewis sat before his study mirrow. Strange to say his eyes wandered continually from the volume in his lap, and he sought his own reflection in the glass before him. Complacently he gazed upon those features, utterly oblivious of the vagaries of statute law or 'All the aches and pains that flesh is heir to.' Up went the hand, and it smoothed back the raven locks from his benevolent forehead. Then, with a smile— (To any one who can finish this sentence correctly, the Pandora offers 1400 shares of stock in J. P. Jones' Mexican project).

name: Jones, J.
motto: He who fights and runs away, will live to fight another day.
description: the White Man's Hope. On Thanksgiving Day, 1910, he fought 'Fiery' Sparks to an eight round draw. On January 24, 1911, he faced 'Fats' Bowen, who forfeited. On February 8, 1911, he lost a 15-round bout for the title of Cliftonia to 'Knock-out' Kelly on points.

name: Kelly
motto: He who fights and runs away, will live to fight another day.
description: the Pride of Cliftonia. On February 8, 1911, he won a 15-round bout for the title of Cliftonia against 'Pug' Jones on points.

name: Yampolski
motto: He who fights and runs away, will live to fight another day.
description: the Russian Terror. On February 3, 1911, he forced 'Green' Horn to take the count in the fifth round.

name: Sparks

name: Reid

description: On February 20, 1911, he won by knockout in the third round against 'Young' Rowland.

name: Bowen
motto: He who fights and runs away, will live to fight another day.
description: On January 24, 1911, he forfeited a match against 'Pug' Jones on the spur of the moment, because of parental objections.

name: Rowland
motto: He who fights and runs away, will live to fight another day.
description: On February 20, 1911, he was knocked out in the third round by 'Kid' Reid.

name: Horn

description: On February 3, 1911, he was forced to take the count in the fifth round by Yampolski.

name: James P. Jones
major: Law Class of 1911, University of Georgia, B.S., B.L.
description: He is my right hand bower. He is absolutely indispensable to this kingdom. He took two degrees at the University of Georgia, B.S. and B.L. His classmates, however, made him strike the B.L. off his list, deeming the former more appropriate. You see he was a clever fellow, he smoked his friends' cigars and drank their whiskey. The distinguishing feature about him is his untiring lip. He directed his energies along the line of rubber manufacturing but resorted to unfair means in the hope of keeping pace with his competitors. He was well advanced on the road to destruction when he stole a bag of peanuts from a blind man and put him on the wrong road home. His mustache, too, is much abused, due, I suppose, to the extreme heat of this climate.

name: Omer Franklin
description: He is the biggest liar in seven states, three territories, and one reservation. He was seen at a large rock-pile where men are hard at work, some at crushing rock, others at paving a long stretch. He was one of the good boys so his punishment was light. When these men left college the question was, not what they would do at practicing law, but what the country was going to do when they invaded it. They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

name: John Fort
description: He was seen at a large rock-pile where men are hard at work, some at crushing rock, others at paving a long stretch. He was one of the good boys so his punishment was light. When these men left college the question was, not what they would do at practicing law, but what the country was going to do when they invaded it. He said that in college they were mere boys who, in spite of anything S. Morris or Fitz Green might have thought to the contrary, were going to succeed in the practice of law, but did not show any disposition to work then, thinking that by a little effort in later days they would be able to bridge the gap over. They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

description: He was seen at a large rock-pile where men are hard at work, some at crushing rock, others at paving a long stretch. He was one of the good boys so his punishment was light. When these men left college the question was, not what they would do at practicing law, but what the country was going to do when they invaded it. They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

When these men left college the question was, not what they would do at practicing law, but what the country was going to do when they invaded it. They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

name: Bob Lumsden
description: He was seen at a large rock-pile where men are hard at work, some at crushing rock, others at paving a long stretch. He was one of the good boys so his punishment was light. When these men left college the question was, not what they would do at practicing law, but what the country was going to do when they invaded it. They handed out this stuff technically called 'hot air' to the unsuspecting; they stole funds belonging to their clients; they robbed.

name: John Sibley
description: Sibley was the victim of circumstances. He cast his lot with a bad crew. Nearby stand voice which sounds familiar: "I'll make a fuss for two bits. That's fair enough." "Why, that is John Sibley and his boy, Robert Jenkins."

name: Robert Jenkins
description: Robert Jenkins didn't have any trouble in making a living because he could naturally out-talk any man in the country. When he was in college he received notice from the executor of his father's will that it would be necessary for him to come home and do some work on the farm for there was a condition precedent to the estate, that he should work six months on the land before he took it over. The only reason the testator did not make it nine months was, he had too much respect for the land.

name: Alsbrook
description: "Skinnie" was an ordinary sort of fellow until he came to college and got to going with Bob Jenkins. After that he got to be a sport. Yet, he was a jolly good fellow. His business at college was unascertained. At one time he looked old "Spot" squarely in the eye and said: "It will cost you one dollar to see what I have. I've got you beat."

name: Strickland
description: "Spot" you know, was the doubting Chancellor. These boys represent the Budweiser Club.

name: Tom Marshall
description: Tom Marshall had but one redeeming feature, that is, he was conversant with sailor brogue. We see first, Tom Marshall, then Graham Wright, Walter Mangum, Jim Palmer and "Sunny Jim" Loyd. The devil then turns and says, "I have not admitted these men absolutely, because I have no need of them. They have no recommendations and display little talent. They are not fit for this place, but there is no other."

name: Graham Wright
description: Graham Wright was serviceable to the extent that he bought a pool room. He was among a long train of men who came by holding their heads high, assuming a considerable degree of dignity.

name: Walter Mangum
description: Mr. Mangum—oh, my—he figured so prominently in politics that he always ran, and the funny part about it is, he never won; then, too, he was over-affectionate in his proffers of friendship and squeezed the medium of exchange from those who came within arms' reach—"I'll take him unconditionally."

name: Jim Palmer
description: Jim Palmer furnished testimony in a case and failed to charge a double fee. He was among a long train of men who came by holding their heads high, assuming a considerable degree of dignity.

name: Loyd
description: "Sunny Jim" Loyd combined magnanimity with under-handed art. He was among a long train of men who came by holding their heads high, assuming a considerable degree of dignity.

name: Millard Rewis
description: Rewis, you know, was saturated with commercialism. The students who came in contact with him will remember how he yearned for the touch of gold. He was a passing good man; but I could not keep him. After some time he was taken away from me and went—I'll bet you can't guess where—to Wingfield's, the idol of his dreams.

name: Jim Broach
description: He is a queer fellow. He was always attempting something, but never accomplished anything, in other words, he was not on the job. He passed by me this morning and I hailed him with "Hello there, Jim Broach." He looked at me a moment, made a characteristic shrug of the shoulders and said, "Why, I don't think I've met you. What is your name?" This makes his presence very undesirable. He told me that the Chancellor was the proper man to issue a distress warrant. I don't like this man. I think he ought to go to the little farm of Whiteacre on the Manor of Dale.

name: Ed Carter
description: Ed and Bill 'shot billiards on a pool table,' something absolutely unpardonable, as Jim Broach will tell you.

name: Bill Gignilliat
description: Ed and Bill 'shot billiards on a pool table,' something absolutely unpardonable, as Jim Broach will tell you.

name: Charlie Adams
description: They tried to set the woods on fire in Rabun county, so I must give them the White Veil Degree.

name: Howell Brooke
description: They tried to set the woods on fire in Rabun county, so I must give them the White Veil Degree.

name: Cicero Marion Dobbs
description: They tried to set the woods on fire in Rabun county, so I must give them the White Veil Degree.

name: Deadwyler, Joseph Lumpkin
clubs: Sec'y. and Treas. of Junior Law Class

name: PERCY MORTON RUBINSTEIN
motto: "Much learning doth make thee mad."
major: Senior Pharmacy Class, A. B., P. G.
clubs: Winner of Wilcox Prize in German, 1910 (shared)

name: JOHN HUBERT FORD
motto: "Know not for knowing's sake."

name: Conklin, Hugh
description: And still the wonder grows how one small hand can carry all his nose.

name: Lanham, Pap
description: Its a bald head that knows its own hair restorer.

name: Creekmore
description: Never laugh at a man with a pug nose, for you don't know what may turn up.

name: Williams, George
description: He is an honest boy but he never speaks without breaking his word.

name: Laird, Robert
description: If you let him tell the story he would fill acres in the field of glory.

name: Witman, Mike
description: My life is one demd horrid grind.

name: Nicolson, Perrin
description: He couldn't carry a tune if it was in a basket.

name: Peacock, Howell
description: Take him all in all, he was a man.

name: Franklin, Omer
description: Great is the glory when the strife is hard.

name: Blitch, Johnnie
description: I have worn out another pair of pants stepping off the sidewalk.

name: Raiford, Bob
description: Poor boy, his jaws are set on ball bearings.

name: Freeman, Hill
description: My feet were a birthday present to me.

name: Troutman, Bob
description: He drank his toast and cracked his joke And Freshmen wondered as he spoke.

name: Lumpkin, Dr. Cook
description: Everything was so still at the pole you could hear a gum drop.

name: Miller, Ral
description: A self-made man who worshiped his maker.

name: Brown, Joe
description: I read your fortune in a fortune book and saw predicted that you would fall heir To money—but I see no signs as yet Except perhaps a little falling hair.

name: Wilson, Wilbe
description: Blessings on the little man Thanks to heaven that you can Still express your inward feelings With an honest, rosy blush.
